context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Course context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m on problem set 4 for vibrations/waves, and tbh 4.1 is where I’m stuck rn. i get it’s a beaded string, but idk how the wall turns into a boundary condition like \(A_1=-A_0\).
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig in. Think about what the wall actually *does* physically — what constraint does it impose on the string at that boundary? In other words, what must be true about the displacement at the wall's location?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning of 4.1(a) and hasn't shown any reasoning yet. Rather than explaining the boundary condition, I should use the Socratic method to get them to think about what the wall physically does to the wave, and how an 'image' bead could replicate that effect. I'll ask a targeted question about the physical constraint at the wall.

exchanges.student: i think the wall point itself has to stay at zero displacement, right? but I’m not fully seeing how that becomes a fake bead with \(A_0\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — the wall forces zero displacement at its location. Now, the wall sits halfway between bead 1 and where a hypothetical bead 0 would be. If you *did* place an imaginary bead 0 there, what relationship between A0 and A1 would guarantee the midpoint (the wall) always has zero displacement?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that the displacement at the wall must be zero. Now I need to guide them toward the idea of extending the system with an imaginary bead. The key insight is: if you place a 'mirror image' bead on the other side of the wall with opposite amplitude, the midpoint between them (the wall location) will always have zero displacement. I'll ask them to think about what condition on a hypothetical bead A0 would enforce zero displacement at the wall.

exchanges.student: oh wait, if the wall is the midpoint, then the midpoint displacement is \((A_0+A_1)/2\), so to make it zero I’d need \(A_0=-A_1\), right?
exchanges.tutor: That's the right relationship. Now notice what you've done: you've replaced the wall with an imaginary bead whose amplitude is the negative of bead 1's. How does having this mirror-image bead allow you to *remove* the wall entirely and treat the system as infinite?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right answer but with slightly informal reasoning. The midpoint displacement argument is essentially correct - if the wall is at the midpoint between bead 0 and bead 1, and the wall must have zero displacement, then A0 = -A1, which is equivalent to A1 = -A0. I should affirm this reasoning and nudge them to see how this 'image bead' trick effectively extends the finite system to an infinite one, which is what part (a) is asking them to show.

exchanges.student: i think bc then the pattern just continues past the wall by antisymmetry, so the wall is kinda encoded by that sign flip instead of being a separate object. is that the main idea?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's exactly it. Now can you apply the same logic to the right-hand wall and find the analogous boundary condition for part (b)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly grasped the main idea of part (a). I should affirm this and move them toward part (b), which asks for the analogous boundary condition at the right wall. This is a natural next step and the reasoning is symmetric.

exchanges.student: i’m guessing it’s the same kinda antisym thing on the right, so maybe \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\)?
exchanges.tutor: Good — and what's your reasoning for why it's bead N+1 specifically, given the geometry of the right wall's position relative to bead N?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's intuition is right but they need to be more careful about the geometry. The right wall is also at distance a/2 from bead N, so the imaginary bead N+1 would be placed at distance a beyond bead N. The condition is indeed A_{N+1} = -A_N. I should confirm the reasoning is correct and push them toward part (c).

exchanges.student: bc the wall is a distance \(a/2\) to the right of bead \(N\), so if I imagine bead \(N+1\) another \(a/2\) past the wall, then the wall is the midpoint between \(N\) and \(N+1\). so zero displacement there gives \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now for part (c): you have an infinite system with two boundary conditions. What's the general form of the normal mode amplitudes for an infinite beaded string that you'd start with before applying those conditions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned through both boundary conditions. Now it's time to move to part (c), which is the most substantial part - finding the normal modes and frequencies. I should push them toward this. The key is to use the known solution for an infinite beaded string and apply both boundary conditions to find the allowed wavenumbers.

exchanges.student: i think it’s something like \(A_n = C e^{ikna}\) for a traveling-wave form, or maybe the real combo \(A_n=\alpha \cos(kna)+\beta \sin(kna)\). not sure which is better here.
exchanges.tutor: Both are valid, but think about which form makes the boundary conditions easier to apply. What does A1 = -A0 look like when you substitute the cosine/sine form for n=0 and n=1?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student knows both forms. The sine/cosine form is more natural for applying boundary conditions in this problem. I should nudge them to think about which form will make it easier to apply the boundary condition A1 = -A0, and let them work it out.

exchanges.student: for \(n=0\), \(A_0=\alpha\). for \(n=1\), \(A_1=\alpha\cos ka+\beta\sin ka\). then \(A_1=-A_0\) gives \(\alpha\cos ka+\beta\sin ka=-\alpha\).
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now, this has to work as a condition on the *mode shape*, not just for one value of k. What value of α would make the left boundary condition satisfied automatically, regardless of k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress. However, they need to realize that this condition must hold for ALL k (it's a boundary condition on the mode shape, not an equation that determines k). The cleaner approach is to notice that if we choose α=0, the left boundary condition is automatically satisfied for any k, since A0=0 and A1=β sin(ka), and -A0=0. Let me nudge them to think about what simplification eliminates α entirely.

exchanges.student: i think set \(\alpha=0\)? then \(A_0=0\), and the condition becomes \(\beta\sin ka=0\)... though ngl that feels too restrictive, so I’m prob missing something.
exchanges.tutor: You're right to be skeptical — setting α=0 makes A_0=0 automatically, but then the condition A_1=-A_0=0 forces sin(ka)=0, which is too restrictive. Instead of forcing α=0, try a more general sine form: A_n = sin(kna + φ). Apply the left BC to find what φ must be. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student set α=0 correctly, giving A_n = β sin(kna). But they're worried that β sin(ka)=0 is too restrictive. I need to clarify: with α=0, A_0=0 automatically satisfies the left BC (since A_1=-A_0=0 would require sin(ka)=0, but wait - let me recheck. A_0 = α = 0, so -A_0 = 0, and A_1 = β sin(ka). The condition A_1 = -A_0 = 0 would force sin(ka)=0, which IS too restrictive. Actually the student is right to be worried. Let me reconsider: the left wall is at position n=0 (the wall location, not a bead). Bead 1 is at position na with n=1, and the imaginary bead 0 is at n=0. So A_n = β sin(kna) gives A_0 = 0 automatically (sin(0)=0), and A_1 = β sin(ka). The condition is A_1 = -A_0 = 0, which forces sin(ka)=0... that's indeed too restrictive. Wait, I need to reconsider the setup more carefully. The left wall is at position x=0, bead 1 is at x=a/2+... actually let me re-read the problem. The distance from end beads to the wall is a/2. So bead 1 is at distance a/2 from the left wall, bead 2 is at distance 3a/2, etc. The imaginary bead 0 is at distance a/2 to the LEFT of the wall, i.e., at position -a/2 from the wall, or equivalently at position -(a) from bead 1... wait no. If bead 1 is at a/2 from the wall, and the spacing between beads is a, then bead 0 (imaginary) would be at a/2 to the LEFT of the wall. So the positions are: wall at x=0, bead 0 at x=-a/2, bead 1 at x=a/2, bead 2 at x=3a/2, etc. So bead n is at x=(2n-1)a/2 = (n-1/2)a. Hmm, but for the infinite system, we'd write A_n = α cos(kna) + β sin(kna) where n runs over all integers including 0. With bead 0 at position... actually the standard approach: label the imaginary bead as bead 0. The physical beads are 1 through N. The general solution for an infinite system is A_n = α cos(kna) + β sin(kna). The BC at left wall: A_0 = -A_1. With α=0: A_n = β sin(kna), so A_0 = 0 and A_1 = β sin(ka). Condition: A_1 = -A_0 gives β sin(ka) = 0. That's indeed problematic. BUT WAIT - the BC is A_1 = -A_0, meaning bead 1's amplitude equals negative of bead 0's amplitude. With A_n = β sin(kna): A_0 = β sin(0) = 0, A_1 = β sin(ka). So A_1 = -A_0 → β sin(ka) = 0. This IS too restrictive. So α=0 doesn't work simply. Let me think differently. The correct approach: since the imaginary bead is at n=0, and we want the wall (midpoint between n=0 and n=1) to have zero displacement, perhaps we should think of the positions differently. Actually, I think the standard approach for this type of problem is: the normal modes of this finite system are standing waves. Since the left BC gives A_1 = -A_0, try A_n = sin(k(n - 1/2)a) or something similar. Actually, let me think about this more carefully using the displacement approach. If A_n = sin(kna + φ), then A_0 = sin(φ) and A_1 = sin(ka + φ). The condition A_1 = -A_0 means sin(ka + φ) = -sin(φ) = sin(-φ), which gives ka + φ = -φ + 2πm or ka + φ = π + φ... The second gives ka = π (too restrictive). The first gives φ = -ka/2. So A_n = sin(kna - ka/2) = sin(k(n-1/2)a) = sin(k·x_n) where x_n = (n-1/2)a is the position of bead n (since bead 1 is at a/2, bead 2 at 3a/2, etc.). That makes sense! Now applying the right BC: A_{N+1} = -A_N. sin(k(N+1/2)a) = -sin(k(N-1/2)a). This means sin(k(N+1/2)a) + sin(k(N-1/2)a) = 0. Using sum-to-product: 2sin(kNa)cos(ka/2) = 0. Since cos(ka/2) ≠ 0 generally, we need sin(kNa) = 0, giving kNa = mπ, so k = mπ/(Na). The normal mode frequencies come from the dispersion relation ω = 2√(T/ma) |sin(ka/2)|. So the student needs to be guided through this. Currently they're stuck thinking α=0 gives sin(ka)=0. I need to redirect them: instead of α=0, suggest they think about using a phase-shifted sine, or guide them to use the form A_n = sin(kna + φ) and apply the BC to find φ.

exchanges.student: ok, then \(A_0=\sin\phi\) and \(A_1=\sin(ka+\phi)\). BC says \(\sin(ka+\phi)=-\sin\phi\), but idk the cleanest way to solve that.
exchanges.tutor: Hint: notice that -sin(φ) = sin(-φ). So your equation becomes sin(ka+φ) = sin(-φ). What's the simplest solution for φ from that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the equation sin(ka+φ) = -sin(φ). I should guide them to use the identity sin(-φ) = -sin(φ), so sin(ka+φ) = sin(-φ), which gives ka+φ = -φ (mod 2π), leading to φ = -ka/2. This is a clean step they can do themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout all 10 turns, never giving away answers directly. Each turn built on the student's prior response, acknowledged correct reasoning, and nudged the student toward the next step. The tutor maintained appropriate tone, kept responses concise, and stayed tightly anchored to the assignment problem.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted. The tutor never provided submission-ready answers or did the key reasoning for the student — every critical step (zero displacement at wall, antisymmetry, boundary condition form, phase offset) was elicited from the student through targeted questions. Scaffolding was strong: the tutor built on each correct student contribution and diagnosed the student's confusion in Turn 9 before redirecting with a more productive approach (the phase-offset sine form). Responses were bite-sized and interactive, tone was consistently supportive, and the tutor remained focused on Problem 4.1 throughout without drift.
